A Data Story Map

Maternal Health
in Idaho [DRAFT]

Following the journey from preconception to postpartum — and the wide county‑to‑county variation hidden beneath statewide averages.

Scroll to explore ↓

Introduction

Why maternal health matters — and how we measure it

A mother’s health is the foundation of a family’s health. Yet maternal outcomes are shaped by far more than clinical care alone — by where a woman lives, how far she must travel, and the social and economic conditions that surround her pregnancy.

Maternal health is measured across the full arc of care: physical conditions like hypertension, diabetes and obesity; mental and emotional health; access to care, including the timing and adequacy of prenatal visits and the distance to a delivery hospital; health behaviors such as tobacco use; and the social drivers — food security, employment, insurance — that quietly set the stage for everything else.

This study consolidates several Idaho datasets into a single dashboard, so indicators from different sources can be read together across one continuous timeline of care. The numbers below come directly from that consolidated data.

Idaho Vital Statistics birth records, 2019–2024 ( births across 44 counties).

Stages of Maternal Health

One continuum, four stages

The study follows maternal health across four connected stages. Each stage is examined through the same five components of health introduced below.

Components of Health

Five lenses on every stage

For each stage of the journey, the study looks at five components that together describe maternal health beyond clinical outcomes alone.

Physical health

Hypertension, diabetes, obesity, BMI.

Mental & emotional health

Depression, stress, social isolation.

Access to healthcare

Transportation, insurance, distance to care, adequacy of prenatal care.

Health behaviors

Tobacco, alcohol, and other substance use.

Social drivers of health

Food insecurity, lost employment, race/ethnicity.

The analytical framework

Stages (columns) × components (rows) form the matrix used throughout the analysis. Each cell shows one representative statewide indicator.

Representative statewide indicators by component of health and stage of the maternal journey.
Preconception Prenatal Delivery Postpartum

BRFSS women 18–49 (2020–2024); Vital Statistics births (2019–2024); PRATS (2023). “—” = not measured at that stage.

Datasets

What this story is built on

Four Idaho data sources are consolidated, each covering different years and geographic levels — an important caveat when comparing across them.

Idaho BRFSS

Women 18–49 · 2020–2024

Public Health District level

Vital Statistics Birth Records

Idaho · 2019–2024

County level

PRATS

Pregnancy Risk Assessment · 2022, 2023

Survey

Emergency Care

Emergency-room encounters · 2022–2025

Encounter level

Data Gaps & Caveats

What the data can’t yet tell us

  • Mismatched geographies. BRFSS is reported by Public Health District (7 regions); births and emergency data are by county (44). Comparisons across sources are approximate.
  • Small-population suppression. In the least-populous counties, single years can have too few births to report reliably; county rates here pool six years (2019–2024) to stabilize them.
  • Lagging and partial years. PRATS covers only 2022–2023; emergency data runs through 2025; survey questions shift year to year.
  • What’s missing. These sources under-capture maternal mortality and severe morbidity, and say little about postpartum care beyond the first weeks.

Observations & Analysis

Statewide averages hide two Idahos

A statewide rate is a single number laid over 44 very different places. Map the same measure county by county and a clear pattern appears: the burden concentrates in Idaho’s rural, sparsely-populated counties. Switch the measure and explore — hover or tap any county.

Patterns Across the State

The rural gradient

Sorting counties into four population classes — Urban, Commuting, Rural Center, and Open Country — the same gradient repeats across nearly every component of health. The most striking gap is the distance women travel for emergency care during pregnancy.

12×

In Idaho’s most remote Open Country counties, of pregnancy-related emergency visits involve a woman traveling 50+ miles from home for care — against just in urban counties.

Five measures, one gradient

Rate by county population class, pooled 2019–2024

Distance to emergency care

Pregnancy-related ER visits with a 50+ mile trip from home

Out-of-hospital birth is rising — fastest in rural Idaho

Share of births occurring outside a hospital, 2019–2024

Access barriers vary by district

Women 18–49 reporting each barrier, by Public Health District (BRFSS)

Who attends the birth is changing too

As births move out of the hospital, the question of who is in the room shifts with them. Physician-attended births have slipped steadily since 2019, while births attended by a licensed midwife have nearly doubled — the clearest sign of a quietly changing model of maternity care in Idaho.

5 pts

Doctor-attended births fell from of all Idaho births in 2019 to in 2024, while licensed-midwife-attended births rose from to — a near-doubling over the same six years.

Birth attendant in Idaho, 2019–2024

Share of all births by attendant type (MD/DO shown on a separate right axis)

County Case Studies

The same journey, four very different places

Statewide, of births involve inadequate prenatal care. But that average dissolves the moment you look county by county. Here are four counties along the urban–rural spectrum, each walked across the maternal journey.

Vital Statistics births, 2019–2024; far-travel from Emergency Care pregnancy visits, 2022–2025. Class = county population characteristic.

Conclusions

What the journey reveals

Read end to end, the data tells one consistent story: in Idaho, geography is a maternal-health risk factor.

The statewide average is a comfortable fiction.

Roughly of Idaho births involve inadequate or no prenatal care statewide — but the rate runs from under 7% in urban Ada County to over 20% in the most rural counties.

Distance is the sharpest divide.

The share of pregnancy-related emergency visits requiring a 50+ mile trip is more than ten times higher in Open Country counties than in urban ones — a direct signal of thinning obstetric coverage.

The model of care is shifting.

Out-of-hospital births have risen every year since 2019, most steeply in rural classes, and the birth-attendant mix has shifted with them — doctor-attended births fell from 85% to 80% while licensed-midwife-attended births nearly doubled, consistent with labor-and-delivery closures and OB-GYN shortages.

Behavioral and social risk track the same map.

Tobacco use in pregnancy, food insecurity, and transportation barriers all rise along the rural gradient, compounding the access gap rather than offsetting it.

Where to go next

Targeting matters more than statewide programs: the counties carrying the highest burden are identifiable and few. Priorities suggested by the data include protecting remaining rural delivery capacity, extending tele-obstetrics and transportation support in Open Country counties, and closing the geographic gaps in the data itself — aligning BRFSS districts with county reporting and extending PRATS coverage so the postpartum stage is as well-measured as the rest.
